expectations.text: Recommends structure-aware or recursive chunking over fixed-size for legal documents
expectations.evidence: 'Use recursive/structure-aware chunking — contracts have explicit structure (sections, clauses, sub-clauses) that must be respected.'

expectations.text: Provides specific chunk size guidance (token range) with overlap percentage
expectations.evidence: 'Target chunk size: 512–1024 tokens with 10-15% overlap.'

expectations.text: Addresses the context-loss problem and recommends contextual retrieval or similar enrichment
expectations.evidence: 'Critical addition — apply Contextual Retrieval (§5)... Before embedding, prepend a context prefix' with a concrete example.

expectations.text: Explains why isolated chunks from legal contracts are ambiguous without document context
expectations.evidence: 'Legal chunks are deeply ambiguous in isolation. "The Party shall indemnify..." means nothing without knowing which contract, which party, which indemnification clause.'

expectations.text: Mentions the retrieval stack framing (chunk layer is first design decision)
expectations.evidence: References retrieval stack sections (§2, §3, §5) throughout, framing chunking as the first design decision in the stack.

expectations.text: Recommends structure-aware or recursive chunking over fixed-size for legal documents
expectations.evidence: 'Structure-aware chunking based on document hierarchy... Split at clause/section boundaries (identified by numbering: "1.", "1.1", "(a)", "Schedule A").'

expectations.text: Provides specific chunk size guidance (token range) with overlap percentage
expectations.evidence: 'Individual clauses: 100-500 tokens (natural boundary)... If a clause is very long (>500 tokens): split at sub-clause or sentence boundaries, with 50-token overlap.'

expectations.text: Addresses the context-loss problem and recommends contextual retrieval or similar enrichment
expectations.evidence: 'Each chunk gets its parent section title prepended' — addresses context loss through metadata enrichment.

expectations.text: Explains why isolated chunks from legal contracts are ambiguous without document context
expectations.evidence: Addresses context loss by recommending heading path metadata: 'Section 3 > Obligations > 3.2 Payment Terms' — implies chunks are ambiguous without this.

expectations.text: Mentions the retrieval stack framing (chunk layer is first design decision)
expectations.evidence: Does not reference the retrieval stack as a design framework; provides a standalone chunking strategy without framing it within a broader retrieval stack.

expectations.text: Uses the retrieval stack as a diagnostic framework to identify the failure layer
expectations.evidence: 'Diagnosis — which layer is failing? The problem is at the retrieval and rerank layers, not chunking.'

expectations.text: Identifies dense-only retrieval as a likely cause of topically-related-but-irrelevant results
expectations.evidence: 'The embedding captures topic similarity but not relevance to the specific question' — identifies dense-only as the root cause.

expectations.text: Recommends hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse) as the fix
expectations.evidence: 'Add hybrid retrieval if not already present (§2). Dense-only misses exact keywords.'

expectations.text: Recommends adding a neural reranker (cross-encoder) for precision improvement
expectations.evidence: 'Add a neural reranker (§2). A cross-encoder sees query + document together and scores relevance, not just embedding similarity.'

expectations.text: Mentions at least one retrieval quality metric (recall@k, MRR, or nDCG) for measuring improvement
expectations.evidence: 'Measure recall@k and MRR on a test set of queries with known-relevant chunks.'

expectations.text: Uses the retrieval stack as a diagnostic framework to identify the failure layer
expectations.evidence: Uses a 'Root causes and fixes' list structure rather than the retrieval stack framework; no mention of retrieval stack layers.

expectations.text: Identifies dense-only retrieval as a likely cause of topically-related-but-irrelevant results
expectations.evidence: 'Your chunks are too broad' and 'No re-ranking' — identifies semantic similarity ≠ relevance as the core problem.

expectations.text: Recommends hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse) as the fix
expectations.evidence: Does not recommend hybrid retrieval; focuses on chunk size, HyDE, reranker, and embedding model — hybrid retrieval not mentioned.

expectations.text: Recommends adding a neural reranker (cross-encoder) for precision improvement
expectations.evidence: 'Add a cross-encoder re-ranker (e.g., `ms-marco-MiniLM` or Cohere Rerank) after initial retrieval.'

expectations.text: Mentions at least one retrieval quality metric (recall@k, MRR, or nDCG) for measuring improvement
expectations.evidence: Does not mention recall@k, MRR, or nDCG; no retrieval quality metrics mentioned.

expectations.text: Confirms that flat retrieval is insufficient for multi-hop/cross-document queries and structured indexing is warranted
expectations.evidence: 'Simple questions work because flat retrieval (§2) handles single-fact lookups well. Complex multi-doc questions fail because they require: Finding information across multiple documents...'

expectations.text: Explains at least two of the three structured indexing options (RAPTOR, GraphRAG, filesystem paradigm)
expectations.evidence: Explains all three: RAPTOR ('zoom in/out' queries), GraphRAG ('connected information' queries), and filesystem paradigm (L0/L1/L2 for navigation).

expectations.text: Recommends the filesystem paradigm (L0/L1/L2) as the best fit for wiki/Confluence/Slack-style corpora
expectations.evidence: 'My recommendation for your case (wiki + Confluence + Slack): start with the filesystem paradigm.'

expectations.text: Notes the critical cross-reference requirement for the filesystem paradigm
expectations.evidence: 'Critical: cross-references (links) between documents must exist. Without them, the agent finds one document but can't navigate to related content.'

expectations.text: Explains why GraphRAG is not the best fit (entity-relationship focus vs document navigation)
expectations.evidence: 'Caveat: loses nuance on conditional/temporal information' for GraphRAG; recommends filesystem paradigm instead for this use case.

expectations.text: Confirms that flat retrieval is insufficient for multi-hop/cross-document queries and structured indexing is warranted
expectations.evidence: 'Simple questions work because the answer lives in one chunk. Complex questions fail because the answer is distributed across multiple documents and requires synthesis.'

expectations.text: Explains at least two of the three structured indexing options (RAPTOR, GraphRAG, filesystem paradigm)
expectations.evidence: Explains document summaries, entity-based indexing, relationship/fact extraction, question decomposition, and knowledge graph augmentation — multiple approaches covered.

expectations.text: Recommends the filesystem paradigm (L0/L1/L2) as the best fit for wiki/Confluence/Slack-style corpora
expectations.evidence: Does not mention the filesystem paradigm (L0/L1/L2) at all; recommends 'document summaries + two-stage retrieval' as first priority.

expectations.text: Notes the critical cross-reference requirement for the filesystem paradigm
expectations.evidence: Does not mention cross-reference requirements; filesystem paradigm not discussed.

expectations.text: Explains why GraphRAG is not the best fit (entity-relationship focus vs document navigation)
expectations.evidence: Does not mention GraphRAG by name; mentions 'knowledge graph' as an option but frames it positively, not as something to avoid for this use case.
